brief_justification: Arrives at the exact correct answer (No) and correctly identifies the Knobe/side-effect effect as the mechanism; goes one level beyond surface by explaining the folk-psychology mechanism and noting the positive-side-effect case resists intentionality attribution, but does not trace second-order effects like why the asymmetry exists or what cognitive heuristics drive it; no explicit monitoring of its own reasoning process, no named biases, no course-correction; no verification attempted — no counterfactual test, no boundary condition check, no re-derivation from a different angle; uses hedging language ('most people resist') appropriately without structured uncertainty analysis or confidence levels; mentions the negative-version contrast as a relevant alternative framing and briefly explains why it differs, but does not evaluate other possible explanations for the No answer; reasoning chain is mostly reproducible — names the phenomenon, states the board's explicit disavowal, concludes folk intuition treats the outcome as a byproduct — but skips the intermediate step of how 'knowing but not intending' maps to typical intentionality judgments.
